name: Proper Error Handling with trap and set -euo pipefail
description: Implement robust error handling in bash scripts using set flags, trap for cleanup, error reporting with line numbers, and safe variable expansion.

steps.title: Enable strict mode
steps.description: Add set flags at the top of every script to catch errors immediately.
steps.command: echo '#!/usr/bin/env bash' > safe_script.sh && echo 'set -euo pipefail' >> safe_script.sh
steps.tip: -e exits on error, -u exits on unset variables, -o pipefail catches failures in pipes.

steps.title: Add an error trap with line number reporting
steps.description: Register a trap that reports the exact line number and command that failed.

steps.title: Add a cleanup trap for temp files
steps.description: Ensure temporary files and resources are cleaned up on exit, error, or interrupt.
steps.command:
cat >> safe_script.sh << 'EOF'

TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
cleanup() {
  rm -rf "$TMPDIR"
  echo "Cleaned up temp directory"
}
trap cleanup EXIT
EOF
steps.warning: EXIT traps run on both success and failure. ERR traps only run on errors. Use EXIT for cleanup and ERR for error reporting.

steps.title: Use safe variable defaults
steps.description: Prevent unset variable errors with default values and explicit checks.
steps.command: echo 'NAME="${1:-default_name}"' && echo 'DIR="${OUTPUT_DIR:=/tmp/output}"' && echo '[[ -z "${API_KEY:-}" ]] && { echo "API_KEY not set" >&2; exit 1; }'
steps.tip: ${var:-default} provides a default without setting the variable. ${var:=default} also assigns the default.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Error Handling Template

# Error reporting with line numbers
on_error() {
  local line=$1
  local cmd=$2
  local code=$?
  echo "" >&2
  echo "=== ERROR ===" >&2
  echo "Script:  $(basename "$0")" >&2
  echo "Line:    $line" >&2
  echo "Command: $cmd" >&2
  echo "Code:    $code" >&2
  echo "=============" >&2
}
trap 'on_error ${LINENO} "${BASH_COMMAND}"' ERR

# Cleanup on exit
TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
cleanup() {
  local exit_code=$?
  rm -rf "$TMPDIR"
  [[ $exit_code -ne 0 ]] && echo "Script failed with code $exit_code" >&2
  exit "$exit_code"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

# Safe variable access
NAME="${1:-}"
[[ -z "$NAME" ]] && { echo "Error: name argument required" >&2; exit 1; }

# Safe command execution
if ! output=$(some_command 2>&1); then
  echo "Command failed: $output" >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "Success: $output"

faq.question: Can I have both ERR and EXIT traps?
faq.answer: Yes. ERR fires when a command fails, EXIT fires when the script exits (for any reason). The ERR trap runs first, then EXIT. Use ERR for error reporting and EXIT for cleanup.

faq.question: How do I intentionally allow a command to fail?
faq.answer: Use 'command || true' to suppress the error, or 'if ! command; then handle_error; fi' for explicit handling. You can also temporarily disable errexit with 'set +e; command; rc=$?; set -e' but this is fragile.

faq.question: What is the difference between ${var:-} and ${var-}?
faq.answer: ${var:-default} uses default if var is unset OR empty. ${var-default} uses default only if var is unset. The colon version (:-) is almost always what you want, as it catches both unset and empty string cases.
